strategic_overview:
The best leverage is to treat the theorem as the von Neumann algebra analogue of the graph-product group argument, not as a generic permanence result for proper proximality. The group proof in Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, Proper Proximality Among Various Families of Groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.02917, Proposition 3.7, works by proving relative proper proximality over every vertex link and then using a finite link-boundary cover. The von Neumann setting has the same combinatorics available through Caspers–Fima, Graph products of operator algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/1411.2799, especially the Fock reduced-word calculus, conditional expectations, and vertex-link AFP decomposition, but the operator-system definition of proper proximality from Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson, Properly Proximal von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517, makes the link-to-absolute upgrade the hard part.

Before attacking proper proximality, reduce the ambient algebra to a very rigid nonamenable factor: Charlesworth–de Santiago–Hayes–Jekel–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Nelson, On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08150, Theorem 2.4 and Theorem E, and Rigid Graph Products, https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.06171, Theorem 8.11, give fullness/factoriality/primeness under essentially the present hypotheses. The proof should then be a contradiction to the existence of an M-central state on S(M). The most promising route is to build link-relative boundary projections from the reduced-word decomposition, prove a relative AFP ping-pong lemma using trace-zero vertex unitaries, and then force any remaining central state into relative amenability to link subalgebras, which is ruled out by the graph-product relative amenability classification in https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08150, Theorem A, or by the relative-amenability intersection theorem in https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.06171, Theorem 5.3.

directions.name: Primary route: link-relative AFP ping-pong plus irreducibility upgrade
directions.description: For each vertex v, write M as the amalgamated free product M_Star(v) *_M_Link(v) M_{V minus {v}}. Let B = M_Link(v), M1 = M_Star(v), M2 = M_{V minus {v}}. Define projections P1 and P2 on L2(M) onto reduced AFP words whose first non-B letter comes from M1 minus B or M2 minus B. Prove these projections lie in the link-relative operator system S_Xv(M), where Xv is the boundary piece generated by e_B. Then imitate the group AFP ping-pong argument: trace-zero unitaries representing distinct graph-product cosets move P1 and P2 into orthogonal subprojections of each other, forcing any central state either to vanish or to be supported on the link boundary. Finally show that such support would give relative amenability to M_Link(v), and rule this out for irreducible graphs with at least three vertices.
key_ideas: The vertex-link decomposition is the exact von Neumann analogue of the group splitting used in the graph-product group proof.
The projections P1 and P2 should be checked in the relative small-at-infinity system, not in S(M) itself; the link-tail error is usually not compact, but should be compact relative to e_M_Link(v).

The trace-zero unitary hypothesis supplies concrete reduced-word unitaries, replacing nontrivial group syllables. If one AFP side has only a two-dimensional centered part, irreducibility should force the other side to have two orthogonal reduced representatives, exactly as in the index condition in the group proof.
Once a central state is forced into link support, use relative amenability/nonamenability results for graph products to eliminate it.
relevant_techniques: Caspers–Fima, Graph products of operator algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/1411.2799, Theorems 2.15 and 2.26: unscrew M as M_Star(v) *_M_Link(v) M_{V minus {v}}.

Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, Proper Proximality Among Various Families of Groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.02917, Corollary 3.2 and Proposition 3.7: direct AFP ping-pong and link-boundary strategy for graph products of groups.

Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson, Properly Proximal von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517, Proposition 6.7: free-product proof via beginning-letter projections; this is the closest existing von Neumann template.
Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517, Lemma 6.1: to prove P1, P2 are in S_Xv(M), check commutators only on the vertex generators/right vertex generators.

On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08150, Lemma 3.17 and Theorem 5.4: relative reduced-word conditional expectation and bimodule decomposition; use these to compute link-relative compactness and orthogonality.

Rigid Graph Products, https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.06171, Lemma 8.10: long reduced trace-zero unitaries show non-intertwining into proper subgraph algebras; adapt the same words to produce the ping-pong unitaries.
directions.potential_obstacles: The main gap is converting the AFP ping-pong into the exact DKEP proper-proximality obstruction. In the relative setting, the projections P1 and P2 may only force a hypothetical state onto the link boundary rather than killing it outright. One then needs a clean lemma saying that link-boundary support of an M-central state yields relative amenability to M_Link(v). Existing theorems proving such a conversion often require mixing or bounded Pimsner-Popa bases, which link inclusions may not satisfy, so a graph-product-specific proof may be needed.

directions.name: Central-state localization using bidual boundary projections
directions.description: Start from a hypothetical M-central state on S(M), pass to the normal bidual model of the small-at-infinity compactification, and analyze its mass on support projections q_Xv associated with the boundary pieces generated by M_Link(v), M_Star(v), or another finite family of subgraph algebras. Prove that nonamenability forces zero mass on the compact support q_K, while the relative ping-pong lemma forces full mass on the finite graph boundary generated by the q_Xv. Then use graph-product relative amenability theorems to show that this mass distribution would imply amenability relative to too many links, hence amenability relative to their intersection, which is C.
key_ideas: Use the bidual projection calculus to replace vague statements about being supported near a link by precise support projections q_X.

For A = M, central projections disappear because M is a factor, so any decomposition theorem becomes a hard dichotomy: either M is properly proximal, or M is amenable relative to one of the chosen subgraph algebras.
Iterating relative amenability over all links collapses to relative amenability over the intersection of all links, which is empty; this contradicts nonamenability.
This direction is the most conceptually faithful to the group proof's finite cover of the Stone-Cech boundary.
relevant_techniques: Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson, Properly Proximal von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517, Theorem 6.2 and Lemma 8.5: central-state and bidual formulations of non-proper-proximality.

Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, Structure of relatively bi-exact group von Neumann algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.05298, Lemmas 3.5, 3.10, and Theorem 1.1: support projections q_X and relative-to-absolute decomposition.

On Relative Biexactness of Amalgamated Free Product von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.19508, Lemmas 5.1 and 5.3: explicit q_X formulas for hereditary boundary pieces generated by Jones projections.
Rigid Graph Products, https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.06171, Theorem 5.3: relative amenability to two subgraph algebras implies relative amenability to their intersection.
On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08150, Theorem A: complete tracial criterion for when M_V1 is amenable relative to M_V2.
directions.potential_obstacles: The off-the-shelf decomposition theorems in https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.05298 and https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.19508 require hypotheses such as mixing inclusions, bounded Pimsner-Popa bases, or relative biexactness. Link subalgebras in graph products are not generally mixing because their stars commute with them. The solver team should expect to adapt the bidual proof using the graph-product bimodule decomposition of https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08150 rather than quote the decomposition theorem verbatim.

directions.name: Direct absolute sector-projection proof
directions.description: Try to avoid relative boundary machinery by constructing a finite family of projections directly in S(M), indexed by graph-combinatorial sectors of reduced words, and derive paradoxical inequalities from trace-zero unitaries along nonedges. A possible sector is: the first letter, in a fixed normal form, that is not allowed to commute past a chosen vertex or clique. The goal is to arrange that right multiplication changes the sector only on finite or compact-in-the-infinity-norm pieces, so that the projections are genuinely in S(M).
key_ideas: This would generalize the proof that free products of diffuse algebras are properly proximal, but with graph-product sectors replacing free-product first-letter sectors.
Use a total order or complement-graph orientation to define canonical first obstruction letters, then prove stability under right multiplication by vertex generators.
Use trace-zero unitaries along nonadjacent vertices to create orthogonal translates of sectors.
Use irreducibility to ensure that no sector can carry an invariant central state without producing a join decomposition.
relevant_techniques: Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson, Properly Proximal von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517, Proposition 6.7: beginning-letter projections in the free-product case.
Caspers–Fima, Graph products of operator algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/1411.2799, Proposition 2.1 and Propositions 2.2–2.3: explicit left/right creation-annihilation formulas and commutant model.
Caspers–Fima, https://arxiv.org/abs/1411.2799, Remarks 2.23–2.24 and Proposition 2.25: conditional expectations onto subgraphs and subgraph intersection formula.
On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08150, Lemmas 3.11–3.13 and Lemma 3.17: relative normal forms and exact expectation formula.
Rigid Graph Products, https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.06171, Lemma 8.8: expectation vanishing for reduced words containing a prescribed subword outside a target subgraph.
directions.potential_obstacles: This is elegant if it works, but it is technically risky. In graph products, right multiplication by a vertex can commute through arbitrarily long link words, so commutators of naive first-letter projections are usually only link-relative compact, not absolutely compact. If these link-tail errors cannot be made compact in the DKEP K^{infty,1}(M) sense, the proof will collapse back into the relative-boundary strategy.

directions.name: Conditional route through biexactness or deformations
directions.description: For special cases, prove M is biexact or relatively biexact and then deduce proper proximality from nonamenability. This is unlikely to solve the stated theorem in full because the vertex algebras are arbitrary separable tracial algebras with no weak exactness or injectivity assumption, but it can provide useful test cases and technical lemmas.
key_ideas: If vertex algebras are weakly exact or injective, use vertex-removal AFP decompositions over links and the recent AFP relative-biexactness technology.

If a word-length deformation or derivation can be built with compact resolvent relative to a graph boundary, apply the DKEP derivation criterion for proper proximality or the DP biexactness criterion.
Use this route to prove the theorem first for finite-dimensional or amenable vertex algebras; the resulting projection and boundary calculations may then be abstracted to arbitrary vertices.
relevant_techniques: Biexact von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.10161, Corollary 6.11: a biexact von Neumann algebra with no amenable direct summand is properly proximal.
Biexact von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.10161, Propositions 6.14 and 6.16: tensor/free-product relative permanence for biexactness.

On Relative Biexactness of Amalgamated Free Product von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.19508, Theorems 4.17, 5.16, and Corollary 5.23: relative/genuine biexactness for AFPs under injectivity or weak exactness plus mixing.
Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson, Properly Proximal von Neumann Algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517, Proposition 9.4: closable derivations with compact resolvent imply proper proximality.
Caspers–Fima, Graph products of operator algebras, https://arxiv.org/abs/1411.2799, Proposition 2.30: graph products of ucp maps; useful for constructing radial length deformations.
directions.potential_obstacles: This route is not sufficient for the theorem as stated. Arbitrary separable tracial vertex algebras need not be weakly exact, injective, or biexact. Also, ordinary word-length resolvents are not compact when vertex centered L2 spaces are infinite-dimensional, so any deformation argument must be relative to subgraph boundary pieces and still needs the hard relative-to-absolute upgrade.

key_obstacles: The central obstacle is that the natural graph-product projections are link-relative rather than absolutely compact: commuting link letters create infinite boundary tails. Therefore the group proof's finite cover of the Stone-Cech boundary does not automatically translate to the DKEP operator system S(M). A second obstacle is the weakness of the trace-zero-unitary hypothesis: it gives nontrivial reduced unitaries but not diffuseness, so arguments relying on weakly-null Haar unitaries or diffuse free-product factors must be replaced by finite reduced-word ping-pong. Third, existing von Neumann relative-to-absolute upgrade theorems often assume mixing inclusions, bounded Pimsner-Popa bases, weak exactness, or injectivity; link and star subalgebras in arbitrary graph products do not obviously satisfy these hypotheses. Finally, the two-dimensional C^2 vertex case is genuinely delicate: it is the von Neumann shadow of the Z/2 exceptional case in graph-product groups, and the proof must use irreducibility of Gamma to rule out all index-two/dihedral-type configurations.
recommended_starting_point: Start with the primary route. The first concrete lemma to prove is the following link-relative AFP sector lemma: for a fixed vertex v, with B = M_Link(v) and M = M_Star(v) *_B M_{V minus {v}}, the projections P1 and P2 onto reduced AFP words beginning in M_Star(v) minus B and M_{V minus {v}} minus B satisfy [Pi, JxJ] in K_Xv^{infty,1}(M) for every vertex-generator x, where Xv is the boundary piece generated by e_B. Use the creation-annihilation formulas from https://arxiv.org/abs/1411.2799 and the generator criterion from https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517, Lemma 6.1. Once that is established, prove the ping-pong inequalities using trace-zero unitaries along reduced graph words, guided by https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.02917, Corollary 3.2, and check the finite C^2 cases against the irreducibility combinatorics. Only after this relative lemma is solid should the team tackle the bidual/link-cover upgrade.
